content.text: Patch embeddings are the step that turns an image into a sequence of tokens so a Vision Transformer can process it like text. Each fixed-size image patch is flattened and projected into a learned vector, and positional information is added so the model knows where each patch came from in the image.
What patch embeddings do

A standard ViT does not look at an image as a 2D grid the way a CNN does. Instead, it splits the image into equal-sized patches, such as 16×16 pixels, and treats each patch as one token in a sequence.
Those patch tokens are then fed into the Transformer encoder, where self-attention can model relationships between distant regions of the image.
How the process works
The workflow is simple:
Split the image into non-overlapping patches.
Flatten each patch into a vector.
Apply a linear projection, or sometimes a small convolution, to map each patch into the model’s embedding dimension.
Add positional embeddings so the sequence preserves spatial order.
Feed the resulting token sequence into the Transformer encoder.
For a 224×224 image with 16×16 patches, you get 14×14=196 patch tokens.
This token count matters because self-attention cost grows quickly as the number of tokens increases.
Why it matters

Patch embeddings are the bridge between images and Transformers. Without them, the model would not have a token sequence to operate on, and it would lose the spatial structure needed for vision tasks.
They also control a major trade-off: smaller patches preserve more detail but increase compute, while larger patches are cheaper but may miss fine-grained information.
Mental model

A useful way to think about patch embeddings is as “word pieces for images.” Just as a language model reads a sentence as a sequence of word tokens, ViT reads an image as a sequence of patch tokens, each representing a local region of visual content.
Why positional embeddings are needed

If the model only saw a list of patch vectors, it would not know which patch was top-left, center, or bottom-right. Positional embeddings solve that by injecting location information into each patch token.
This is essential because images are spatial data, and location often changes meaning: an eye patch and a shoe patch are not interchangeable even if their raw pixel statistics look similar.
Design choices

The patch size is one of the most important ViT hyperparameters. - Smaller patches give finer spatial detail but more tokens and higher cost. - Larger patches reduce compute but can blur away small structures like text or tiny objects. - Variable-resolution methods and multi-scale patch embeddings try to improve flexibility when images do not fit one fixed size well.
In generative AI

In generative AI, patch embeddings are often the visual front end for multimodal systems. They convert images into a format that can be fused with language tokens or other modalities, enabling tasks like captioning, visual question answering, and image-conditioned generation.
That is why patch embedding is not just a preprocessing trick; it is a core architectural choice that shapes what the model can see, how much detail it retains, and how expensive inference becomes.
Common pitfalls
Using patches that are too large for the task, which can hide small details.
Forgetting positional embeddings, which removes spatial order.
Resizing every image blindly, which can hurt resolution-sensitive tasks.
Assuming patch embedding alone captures semantics; most meaning emerges later through Transformer layers.
Architecture — Patch Embedding Pipeline

attrs.alt: Patch Embedding Pipeline — image to token sequence with positional embeddings

content.text: The Mathematics of Patch Embeddings
Given an input image
(height, width, channels), the patch embedding step produces a token sequence that the Transformer encoder can process. Let
denote the patch size. The image is divided into
non-overlapping patches.
Each patch
is produced by flattening the patch pixels. A learnable linear projection
maps each flattened patch to the model's embedding dimension

attrs.latex: z_0 = [x_{\text{class}}; x_1^p W_E; x_2^p W_E; \ldots; x_N^p W_E] + E_{\text{pos}}

content.text: where
is a learnable classification token prepended to the sequence (following BERT), and
is a learnable positional embedding matrix. The full sequence length is
The self-attention cost scales quadratically with sequence length:

For a 224×224 image with P=16, N=196, giving O(196² × D) ≈ 38K × D operations per attention layer. With P=8, N=784, the cost is O(784²) ≈ 614K × D — a 16× increase for 4× smaller patches. This quadratic scaling is the dominant motivation for choosing the patch size.
Benefits of Patch Embeddings
Architecture unification.
Patch embeddings allow images to be processed by the same Transformer architecture used for text, enabling parameter sharing and multimodal fusion without architectural surgery.
Global receptive field from layer one.
Unlike CNN convolutions which are local, self-attention after patch embedding allows every patch to attend to every other patch from the first Transformer layer.
Scalability.
Vision Transformers scale predictably with model size and data — empirically, ViT performance improves log-linearly with compute budget, matching the scaling behavior of language models.
Transfer learning.
Patch embeddings and positional embeddings can be interpolated to handle images of different resolutions at fine-tuning time, enabling flexible transfer to downstream tasks.
Simplicity.
Compared to complex multi-scale CNN feature extraction, patch embedding is a single linear layer — easy to implement, analyse, and modify.
Trade-offs
Data hunger.

ViTs without strong inductive biases need significantly more data than CNNs to learn competitive visual representations. ViT-B trained on ImageNet-1K from scratch underperforms ResNet-50; it requires JFT-300M or aggressive data augmentation (DeiT).
Quadratic attention cost.

Self-attention is O(N²) in the sequence length. For high-resolution images (512×512 with P=16, N=1024), this becomes expensive. Efficient attention variants (Linformer, Swin windowed attention) mitigate this.
No built-in locality prior.

CNNs are inductive biased toward local features (edges, textures) due to convolutions. ViTs must learn these from data, requiring more training examples or augmentation to match CNN performance on small datasets.
Patch boundary artifacts.

Features that span patch boundaries may be harder for the model to integrate, since patches are processed independently before attention. Overlapping patches or convolution stems partially address this.
Fixed resolution assumption.
Learned positional embeddings are trained at a specific resolution. Applying the model to images of very different resolutions requires positional embedding interpolation, which degrades performance.
How to Implement Patch Embeddings
The following is a complete, minimal PyTorch implementation of the ViT patch embedding layer:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math


class PatchEmbedding(nn.Module):
    """
    Split an image into fixed-size patches and project each to D dimensions.

    Args:
        image_size:  H = W (square images assumed)
        patch_size:  P (must divide image_size evenly)
        in_channels: number of image channels (3 for RGB)
        embed_dim:   D, the Transformer model dimension
        dropout:     applied to the final embedding
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        image_size: int = 224,
        patch_size: int = 16,
        in_channels: int = 3,
        embed_dim: int = 768,
        dropout: float = 0.0,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        assert image_size % patch_size == 0, "image_size must be divisible by patch_size"

        self.patch_size  = patch_size
        self.num_patches = (image_size // patch_size) ** 2
        patch_dim        = in_channels * patch_size * patch_size

        # Linear projection of flattened patches
        self.projection = nn.Linear(patch_dim, embed_dim)

        # Learnable positional embeddings — includes a slot for [CLS]
        self.cls_token  = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, 1, embed_dim))
        self.pos_embed  = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, self.num_patches + 1, embed_dim))
        self.dropout    = nn.Dropout(dropout)

        self._init_weights()

    def _init_weights(self):
        nn.init.trunc_normal_(self.pos_embed, std=0.02)
        nn.init.trunc_normal_(self.cls_token, std=0.02)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        x: (B, C, H, W)
        returns: (B, N+1, D)  where N = num_patches
        """
        B, C, H, W = x.shape
        P = self.patch_size

        # Step 1: rearrange into patches (B, N, P*P*C)
        x = x.unfold(2, P, P).unfold(3, P, P)  # (B, C, H/P, W/P, P, P)
        x = x.permute(0, 2, 3, 1, 4, 5)        # (B, H/P, W/P, C, P, P)
        x = x.contiguous().view(B, -1, C * P * P)  # (B, N, patch_dim)

        # Step 2: linear projection
        x = self.projection(x)                  # (B, N, D)

        # Step 3: prepend [CLS] token
        cls = self.cls_token.expand(B, -1, -1)  # (B, 1, D)
        x   = torch.cat([cls, x], dim=1)        # (B, N+1, D)

        # Step 4: add positional embeddings
        x = x + self.pos_embed                  # (B, N+1, D)
        return self.dropout(x)


# ── Demonstration ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == "__main__":
    patch_emb = PatchEmbedding(image_size=224, patch_size=16, embed_dim=768)

    batch = torch.randn(4, 3, 224, 224)   # batch of 4 RGB images
    out   = patch_emb(batch)
    print(f"Input shape:  {batch.shape}")
    print(f"Output shape: {out.shape}")   # expected: (4, 197, 768)
    print(f"Num patches:  {patch_emb.num_patches}")  # 196

    total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in patch_emb.parameters())
    print(f"Patch embed parameters: {total_params:,}")  # ≈ 593K for D=768
Examples
Example 1: Positional embedding interpolation for variable resolution
When fine-tuning ViT on images of a different size, positional embeddings must be interpolated:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


def interpolate_pos_embed(
    pos_embed: torch.Tensor,
    num_patches_new: int,
    orig_size: int,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Interpolate position embeddings from original grid size to new grid size.

    pos_embed:        (1, N_orig+1, D) — includes the [CLS] token at index 0
    num_patches_new:  target N (for new image size / patch size)
    orig_size:        sqrt(N_orig) — original grid side length
    """
    cls_token_embed  = pos_embed[:, :1, :]          # (1, 1, D)
    patch_embeds     = pos_embed[:, 1:, :]           # (1, N_orig, D)
    D = pos_embed.shape[-1]

    # Reshape to 2D spatial grid: (1, orig_size, orig_size, D) → (1, D, h, w)
    patch_embeds = patch_embeds.reshape(1, orig_size, orig_size, D)
    patch_embeds = patch_embeds.permute(0, 3, 1, 2).float()

    # New grid size
    new_size = int(num_patches_new ** 0.5)

    # Bilinear interpolation
    patch_embeds = F.interpolate(
        patch_embeds, size=(new_size, new_size), mode="bilinear", align_corners=False
    )
    patch_embeds = patch_embeds.permute(0, 2, 3, 1).reshape(1, new_size * new_size, D)

    return torch.cat([cls_token_embed, patch_embeds], dim=1)


# Example: ViT-B/16 trained on 224x224 (14x14 grid) → fine-tune on 384x384 (24x24 grid)
D         = 768
orig_pos  = torch.randn(1, 197, D)   # 196 patch tokens + 1 CLS
new_pos   = interpolate_pos_embed(orig_pos, num_patches_new=576, orig_size=14)
print(f"Original pos embed: {orig_pos.shape}")   # (1, 197, 768)
print(f"Interpolated:       {new_pos.shape}")    # (1, 577, 768)
Example 2: Convolutional patch embedding (equivalent but faster)
The linear projection can be replaced by a single strided convolution, which is mathematically equivalent but more GPU-efficient due to optimised CUDA kernels:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn


class ConvPatchEmbedding(nn.Module):
    """
    Equivalent to linear patch embedding but implemented with a strided Conv2d.
    This is the approach used in the original ViT paper for efficiency.
    """

    def __init__(self, image_size=224, patch_size=16, in_channels=3, embed_dim=768):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_patches = (image_size // patch_size) ** 2
        # stride=patch_size, kernel=patch_size is equivalent to flatten+linear
        self.conv = nn.Conv2d(in_channels, embed_dim,
                              kernel_size=patch_size, stride=patch_size)
        self.cls_token = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, 1, embed_dim))
        self.pos_embed = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, self.num_patches + 1, embed_dim))

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        B = x.shape[0]
        x = self.conv(x)                          # (B, D, H/P, W/P)
        x = x.flatten(2).transpose(1, 2)          # (B, N, D)
        cls = self.cls_token.expand(B, -1, -1)
        x   = torch.cat([cls, x], dim=1) + self.pos_embed
        return x


# Compare outputs (should match linear version within floating-point precision
# when weights are manually aligned)
model1 = ConvPatchEmbedding()
img    = torch.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
out    = model1(img)
print(f"Conv patch embed output: {out.shape}")  # (2, 197, 768)
Real-World Example

In production vision-language models like CLIP and LLaVA, patch embeddings are the interface between the image encoder and the language model. The following shows how patch embeddings integrate in a minimal CLIP-style contrastive model:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class MiniVisionEncoder(nn.Module):
    """
    A minimal ViT-style vision encoder demonstrating patch embeddings
    in a multimodal contrastive learning context.
    """

    def __init__(self, image_size=224, patch_size=16, embed_dim=512, depth=6, num_heads=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.patch_embed = ConvPatchEmbedding(image_size, patch_size, 3, embed_dim)
        encoder_layer    = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            d_model=embed_dim, nhead=num_heads, dim_feedforward=embed_dim * 4,
            batch_first=True, norm_first=True,  # pre-norm (more stable)
        )
        self.encoder     = nn.TransformerEncoder(encoder_layer, num_layers=depth)
        self.norm        = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Returns the [CLS] token representation as the image embedding."""
        x = self.patch_embed(x)   # (B, N+1, D)
        x = self.encoder(x)       # (B, N+1, D)
        x = self.norm(x[:, 0])    # (B, D) — CLS token only
        return F.normalize(x, dim=-1)  # unit norm for cosine similarity


class MiniTextEncoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size=32000, embed_dim=512, depth=6, num_heads=8, max_len=77):
        super().__init__()
        self.token_embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
        self.pos_embed   = nn.Embedding(max_len, embed_dim)
        encoder_layer    = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(d_model=embed_dim, nhead=num_heads,
                                                       batch_first=True, norm_first=True)
        self.encoder     = nn.TransformerEncoder(encoder_layer, num_layers=depth)
        self.norm        = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)

    def forward(self, tokens: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, L = tokens.shape
        pos  = torch.arange(L, device=tokens.device).unsqueeze(0)
        x    = self.token_embed(tokens) + self.pos_embed(pos)
        x    = self.encoder(x)
        x    = self.norm(x.mean(dim=1))   # mean-pooled text representation
        return F.normalize(x, dim=-1)


# Contrastive loss (CLIP-style)
def clip_loss(image_feats: torch.Tensor, text_feats: torch.Tensor, temp: float = 0.07) -> torch.Tensor:
    logits = image_feats @ text_feats.T / temp   # (B, B)
    labels = torch.arange(logits.shape[0], device=logits.device)
    return (F.cross_entropy(logits, labels) + F.cross_entropy(logits.T, labels)) / 2


# Instantiate and run
class ConvPatchEmbedding(nn.Module):  # redeclare to make script self-contained
    def __init__(self, image_size=224, patch_size=16, in_channels=3, embed_dim=512):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_patches = (image_size // patch_size) ** 2
        self.conv        = nn.Conv2d(in_channels, embed_dim, kernel_size=patch_size, stride=patch_size)
        self.cls_token   = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, 1, embed_dim))
        self.pos_embed   = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, self.num_patches + 1, embed_dim))
    def forward(self, x):
        B  = x.shape[0]
        x  = self.conv(x).flatten(2).transpose(1, 2)
        x  = torch.cat([self.cls_token.expand(B, -1, -1), x], dim=1) + self.pos_embed
        return x

vision  = MiniVisionEncoder(embed_dim=512, depth=2, num_heads=8)
text_enc= MiniTextEncoder(embed_dim=512, depth=2, num_heads=8)

images  = torch.randn(4, 3, 224, 224)
tokens  = torch.randint(0, 32000, (4, 20))

img_feats  = vision(images)
txt_feats  = text_enc(tokens)
loss       = clip_loss(img_feats, txt_feats)
print(f"Image features: {img_feats.shape}, Text features: {txt_feats.shape}")
print(f"Contrastive loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
Best Practices
Choose patch size based on the task.
Use P=16 as the default. For tasks requiring fine-grained detail (OCR, small object detection), use P=8 at higher compute cost. For large-scale pretraining on limited compute, P=32 is faster.
Use a convolutional stem for small datasets.
If training from scratch on fewer than 100K images, replace the linear patch embedding with a few CNN layers before the Transformer. The resulting hybrid ViT converges faster and generalises better.
Interpolate positional embeddings when fine-tuning at different resolutions.
Never discard pretrained positional embeddings. Bilinear interpolation of the 2D position embedding grid retains most of the pretrained spatial knowledge.
Normalise inputs.
Always normalise image pixels to zero-mean, unit variance per channel using ImageNet statistics (mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]) when using pretrained ViT weights.
Use pre-norm (LN before attention).
Pre-LayerNorm Transformers are more stable during training than post-LN variants, especially for large models.
Do not freeze patch embeddings when fine-tuning.
Unlike CNN early layers which learn general edge detectors, ViT patch embeddings are closely coupled to the positional embeddings and should be updated during fine-tuning.
Performance and Scalability

The computational cost of the patch embedding layer itself is negligible — it is a single matrix multiplication of shape (B × N × P²C) × (P²C × D). The dominant cost is the Transformer encoder's self-attention, which scales as O(N² × D) per layer.

For high-resolution inputs, the N² scaling becomes prohibitive. A 1024×1024 image with P=16 gives N=4096 — at this scale, attention requires O(16M) operations per layer, making full self-attention impractical. Efficient alternatives:
Window attention (Swin Transformer).
Attention restricted to local windows of size M×M, reducing cost from O(N²) to O(M²N).
Factorised attention (Axial-ViT).
Separate row and column attention, reducing O(N²) to O(N^{3/2}).
Token merging (ToMe).
Progressively merge redundant patches through the layers, reducing effective N as depth increases.
Sparse attention.
Attend only to the most relevant patches (e.g., based on gradient magnitude), reducing compute for tasks where not all patches are equally informative.
ViT-B/16
ViT-L/16
ViT-B/32
Swin-B
DeiT-B/16

content.text: Comparison with Alternatives
Patch embeddings are one of several ways to convert images into sequences for Transformer processing:
Flat patch (ViT)

content: Divide into PxP patches, linear project
Needs large data; quadratic attention cost

content.text: CNN feature map tokens (Hybrid ViT)

content: CNN front-end, then treat feature map cells as tokens

content.text: Window attention (Swin)
Convolutional stem + patch
Standard CNN (no tokens)

content: Strong inductive bias; low data needs
Fixed receptive field; limited global context

content.text: Security and Reliability

Patch embeddings in vision models are subject to adversarial attacks at the patch level. A class of attacks called patch attacks places a small printed adversarial patch in one region of an image to fool the model on the entire image. Because ViT self-attention allows every patch to attend to every other patch, a single adversarial patch can corrupt the representations of all other patches through attention. CNN-based models are somewhat more robust to this because their receptive fields are local.

For deployment of ViTs in safety-critical applications (autonomous vehicles, medical imaging), adversarial patch robustness should be evaluated explicitly. Certified defences such as patch smoothing (detecting and masking adversarial patches) and adversarial training on patch attacks are the main mitigations.
Adversarial patches:
Small region of adversarial pixels can corrupt the full image representation via global attention. Use patch masking or adversarial training for robustness.
Distribution shift:
ViTs pretrained on natural images may fail on medical, satellite, or microscopy images. Domain adaptation fine-tuning is required.
Positional embedding brittleness:
Models are sensitive to the absolute position of patches. Significant image crops or padding can degrade predictions. Use flexible position encoding schemes for production.
Key takeaways
Patch embeddings convert an image into a sequence of learnable tokens.
d2l
They make ViT possible by adapting images to Transformer input format.
Patch size trades off detail versus compute.
Positional embeddings preserve spatial layout.
In generative AI, they are a foundational step for multimodal vision-language models.
